concepts.title: ILATE Rule for Integration
concepts.conceptBody:
ILATE Rule for Integration

Integral of product of two functions

$=\left( {{1}^{\text{st }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right.$ function $)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (Integral of ${{2}^{\text{nd }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ function $)-$ Integral of $\left\{ \left( {} \right. \right.$ differential of ${{1}^{\text{st }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ function $)$ Integral of ${{2}^{\text{nd }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ function}

In symbols: 

$\int ~f\left( x \right)\cdot g\left( x \right)dx=f\left( x \right)\cdot \int ~g\left( x \right)dx-\int ~\left\{ \frac{d}{dx}f\left( x \right)\cdot \int ~g\left( x \right)dx \right\}dx$

Or $\int ~u\cdot vdx=u\int ~vdx-\int ~{u}'\left( \int ~vdx \right)dx$ 

Working Rule: 

(i) Integrals of functions of the form uv may be found by method of integration by parts when $\text{u}$ and $\text{v}$ are two different functions not connected by their derivatives.

In finding integrals by this method proper choice of functions $u$ and $v$ is essential. Although there is no fixed law for taking $\text{u}$ and $\text{v}$ and their choice is possible by practice, yet following rule is helpful in the choice of functions $u$ and $v$.

If the two functions are different types, take that functions as, $u$ which comes first in the word ILATE. 

where I stands for Inverse circular function

$\mathbf{L}$ stands for Logarthmic function

A stands for Algebraic function

$\mathbf{T}$ stands for Trigonometrical functions

and $\mathbf{E}$ stands for Exponential function

(ii) If both the functions are trigonometrical, take that function as $\text{v}$ whose integral is simpler.

(iii) If both the functions are algebraic take that functions as $u$ whose $d\text{c}$ is simpler.

Note: 

  1. Integral can be used by the method of integration by parts if the integrand is the product of two functions $u$ and $v$, where $d\text{c}$ of $\text{u}$ is not a factor of $\text{v}$ and d.c. of $\text{v}$ is not a factor of $\text{u}$.
  2. If logarithmic or inverse circular function occurs in the numerator and its d.c. is not a factor of the integrand use integration by parts.
  3. Sometimes integration by parts may be used by taking integrand as $u$ and 1 as $v$.

concepts.title: Two Standard Integrals where the integrand is in the form of e^x[f(x)+f'(x)] and [xf'(x)+f(x)]
concepts.conceptBody:
Two Standard Integrals where the integrand is in the form of e^x[f(x)+f'(x)] and [xf'(x)+f(x)]

(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{e}}^{\text{az}}}\text{cos}\left( \text{bx}+\text{c} \right)$ (ii) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ eaz $\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)$

Applying the rule of integration by parts, we obtain

$~=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)-\int ~-\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}b\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$~=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+\frac{b}{a}\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ … (i)

Similarly we have $\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$~=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-\int ~\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\cdot b\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$~=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-\frac{b}{a}\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ … (ii)

If the value of $\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ be required, we substitute the R.H.S. of (ii) for the last term of (i) and if the value of $\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ be required, we substitute the R.H.S. of (i) for the last term of (ii). In the former case we get

$~\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+\frac{b}{{{a}^{2}}}{{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\left( 1+\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}} \right)\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$~={{e}^{az}}\frac{a\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+b\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)}{{{a}^{2}}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $~\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{a\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+b\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)}{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

Similarly, we have

$\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{a\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-b\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)}{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

To put the results in another form, we determine two numbers $r$ and a such that

$a=r\text{cos}\alpha \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }b=r\text{sin}\alpha $

These give

$r=\left( {{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}} \right),\alpha =\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( b/a \right)$

$\therefore $ $~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{r\text{cos}\left( bx+c-a \right)}{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

$~={{e}^{az}}\frac{\text{cos}\left( bx+c-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{b}{a} \right)}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}$

Similarly

$\int ~{{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( bx+c-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{b}{a} \right)}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}$

Illustration 32 

Evaluate 

(i) $\int {{\text{e}}^{3\text{x}}}\text{sin}4\text{x}d\text{x}$, 
(ii) $\int {{e}^{4x}}\text{cos}2x\text{cos}4xdx$, 

(iii) $\int x{{e}^{2x}}\text{cos}xdx$ 

Solution: 

(i) From the formula proved above in we get

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}
\int ~{{e}^{3x}}\text{sin}4xdx & ~=\frac{{{e}^{3z}}}{\sqrt{\left( {{3}^{2}}+{{4}^{2}} \right)}}\text{sin}\left( 4x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{4}{3} \right) \\
{} & ~=\frac{{{e}^{2x}}}{5}\text{sin}\left( 4x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{4}{3} \right) \\
\end{array}$

(ii) Now, $\text{cos}2x\text{cos}4x=\frac{1}{2}\left[ 2\text{cos}2x\text{cos}4x \right]$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left( \text{cos}6x+\text{cos}2x \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}\text{cos}2\text{xcos}4\text{x}d\text{x}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}\text{cos}6\text{x}d\text{x}+\frac{1}{2}\int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}\text{cos}2\text{x}d\text{x}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{z}}}}{\sqrt{{{4}^{2}}+{{6}^{2}}}}\text{cos}\left( 6\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{6}{4} \right)+\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}}{\sqrt{\left( {{4}^{2}}+{{2}^{2}} \right)}}\text{cos}\left( 2\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{2}{4} \right)$

$=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}}{2}\left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{52}}\text{cos}\left( 6\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{3}{2} \right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{20}}\text{cos}\left( 2\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right) \right]$

(iii) To evaluate $\int \text{x}{{\text{e}}^{2\text{x}}}\text{cosx}d\text{x}$, we apply the rule of integration by parts. Taking $\text{x}$ and ${{\text{e}}^{2\text{z}}}\text{cos}$ $\text{x}$ as two factors, we have

using the formula in this case also

\[\int ~\underbrace{x}_{I}\underbrace{{{e}^{2x}}\cos x}_{II}\]\[~=x\int ~{{e}^{2x}}\text{cos}xdx-\int ~1\cdot \int ~{{e}^{2x}}\text{cos}xdx\]

\[~=x\cdot \frac{{{e}^{2z}}}{\sqrt{5}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)-\int ~1\cdot \frac{{{e}^{2x}}}{\sqrt{5}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)dx\]

Again,

$~\int ~{{e}^{2z}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)dx$

$~=\int ~x{{e}^{2z}}\text{cos}xdx={{e}^{2z}}\left[ \frac{x}{\sqrt{5}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)-\frac{1}{5}\text{cos}\left( x-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right) \right]$

Illustration 34 

Evaluate $\int \text{sin}\left( \text{log}x \right)dx$ 

Solution: 

[Here integrand contains, expression of the form $\text{sin}\theta $, where $\theta =\text{logx}$ which is a function of $\text{x}$; therefore, put $\text{z}=\text{logx}$.]

Let $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ z}=\text{logx};\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ then $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dz}=\frac{1}{\text{x}}\text{dx}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dz}=\frac{\text{dx}}{{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\text{dz}=\text{dx }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ as $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{x}=\text{z }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}=\text{x}$

Now, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int \text{sin}\left( \text{log}x \right)dx=\int \left( \text{sin}z \right){{e}^{z}}dz$

$~=\text{sin}z\cdot {{e}^{z}}-\int ~\text{cos}z\cdot {{e}^{z}}dz$

$~={{e}^{z}}\text{sin}z-\left[ {{e}^{z}}\text{cos}z-\int ~\left( -\text{sin}z \right){{e}^{z}}dz \right]$

$~={{e}^{z}}\text{sin}z-{{e}^{z}}\text{cos}z-\int ~{{e}^{z}}\text{sin}zdz$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}={{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\left( \text{sinz}-\text{cosz} \right)-\text{I}$ $\left[ \because \int ~{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\text{sinzdz}=\text{I} \right]$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{I}=\left( {{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{sin}z-\text{cos}z \right) \right.$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\frac{ez}{2}\left( \text{sin}z-\text{cos}z \right)+c$

$=\frac{x}{2}\left[ \text{sin}\left( \text{log}x \right)-\text{cos}\left( \text{log}x \right) \right]+c$

$\left[ \therefore {{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}=\text{x} \right.$ and $\left. \text{logx}=\text{z} \right]$

concepts.title: Solving Integrals where the integrand is in the form of e^(ax)[cos (bx+c)] or e^(ax)[sin (bx+c)]
concepts.conceptBody:
Solving Integrals where the integrand is in the form of e^(ax)[cos (bx+c)] or e^(ax)[sin (bx+c)] 

(i) ${{\text{e}}^{\text{az}}}\text{cos}\left( \text{bx}+\text{c} \right)$ (ii) ${{\text{e}}^{\text{az}}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)$

Applying the rule of integration by parts, we obtain

$=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)-\int -\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}b\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+\frac{b}{a}\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ … (i)

Similarly we have $\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-\int \frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\cdot b\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$=\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-\frac{b}{a}\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ … (ii)

If the value of $\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ be required, we substitute the R.H.S. of (ii) for the last term of (i) and if the value of $\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx$ be required, we substitute the R.H.S. of (i) for the last term of (ii). In the former case we get

$\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$\frac{{{e}^{az}}}{a}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+\frac{b}{{{a}^{2}}}{{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$\Rightarrow\left( 1+\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}} \right)\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx$

$={{e}^{az}}\frac{a\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+b\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)}{{{a}^{2}}}$

$\Rightarrow\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{a\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)+b\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)}{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

Similarly, we have

$\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{a\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)-b\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)}{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

To put the results in another form, we determine two numbers $r$ and a such that

$a=r\text{cos}\alpha \text{ and }b=r\text{sin}\alpha $

These give

$r=\left( {{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}} \right),\alpha =\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( b/a \right)$

$\therefore\int {{e}^{az}}\text{cos}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{r\text{cos}\left( bx+c-a \right)}{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

$={{e}^{az}}\frac{\text{cos}\left( bx+c-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{b}{a} \right)}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}$

Similarly

$\int {{e}^{az}}\text{sin}\left( bx+c \right)dx={{e}^{az}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( bx+c-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{b}{a} \right)}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}$

Illustration 

Evaluate 

(i) $\int {{\text{e}}^{3\text{x}}}\text{sin}4\text{x}d\text{x}$, 
(ii) $\int {{e}^{4x}}\text{cos}2x\text{cos}4xdx$, 

(iii) $\int x{{e}^{2x}}\text{cos}xdx$ 

Solution: 

(i) From the formula proved above in we get

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \int ~{{e}^{3x}}\text{sin}4xdx & ~=\frac{{{e}^{3z}}}{\sqrt{\left( {{3}^{2}}+{{4}^{2}} \right)}}\text{sin}\left( 4x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{4}{3} \right) \\ {} & ~=\frac{{{e}^{2x}}}{5}\text{sin}\left( 4x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{4}{3} \right) \\ \end{array}$

(ii) Now, $\text{cos}2x\text{cos}4x=\frac{1}{2}\left[ 2\text{cos}2x\text{cos}4x \right]$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left( \text{cos}6x+\text{cos}2x \right)$

$\therefore \int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}\text{cos}2\text{xcos}4\text{x}d\text{x}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}\text{cos}6\text{x}d\text{x}+\frac{1}{2}\int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}\text{cos}2\text{x}d\text{x}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{z}}}}{\sqrt{{{4}^{2}}+{{6}^{2}}}}\text{cos}\left( 6\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{6}{4} \right)+\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}}{\sqrt{\left( {{4}^{2}}+{{2}^{2}} \right)}}\text{cos}\left( 2\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{2}{4} \right)$

$=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}}}}{2}\left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{52}}\text{cos}\left( 6\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{3}{2} \right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{20}}\text{cos}\left( 2\text{x}-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right) \right]$

(iii) To evaluate $\int \text{x}{{\text{e}}^{2\text{x}}}\text{cosx}d\text{x}$, we apply the rule of integration by parts. Taking $\text{x}$ and ${{\text{e}}^{2\text{z}}}\text{cos}$ $\text{x}$ as two factors, we have

using the formula in this case also

$\int ~\underbrace{x}_{I}\underbrace{{{e}^{2x}}\cos x}_{II} =x\int ~{{e}^{2x}}\text{cos}xdx-\int ~1\cdot \int ~{{e}^{2x}}\text{cos}xdx$

$~=x\cdot \frac{{{e}^{2z}}}{\sqrt{5}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)-\int ~1\cdot \frac{{{e}^{2x}}}{\sqrt{5}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)dx$

Again,

$~\int ~{{e}^{2z}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)dx$

$~=\int ~x{{e}^{2z}}\text{cos}xdx={{e}^{2z}}\left[ \frac{x}{\sqrt{5}}\text{cos}\left( x-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right)-\frac{1}{5}\text{cos}\left( x-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{1}{2} \right) \right]$

Illustration 

Evaluate $\int \text{sin}\left( \text{log}x \right)dx$ 

Solution: 

[Here integrand contains, expression of the form $\text{sin}\theta $, where $\theta =\text{logx}$ which is a function of $\text{x}$; therefore, put $\text{z}=\text{logx}$.]

Let $\text{ z}=\text{logx};$ then $\text{ dz}=\frac{1}{\text{x}}\text{dx}$

or $\text{ dz}=\frac{\text{dx}}{{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}}$ or ${{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\text{dz}=\text{dx }$ as $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{x}=\text{z }\therefore {{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}=\text{x}$

Now, $I=\int \text{sin}\left( \text{log}x \right)dx=\int \left( \text{sin}z \right){{e}^{z}}dz$

$=\text{sin}z\cdot {{e}^{z}}-\int \text{cos}z\cdot {{e}^{z}}dz$

$={{e}^{z}}\text{sin}z-\left[ {{e}^{z}}\text{cos}z-\int \left( -\text{sin}z \right){{e}^{z}}dz \right]$

$={{e}^{z}}\text{sin}z-{{e}^{z}}\text{cos}z-\int {{e}^{z}}\text{sin}zdz$

$\therefore \text{ I}={{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\left( \text{sinz}-\text{cosz} \right)-\text{I}$ $\left[ \because \int {{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\text{sinzdz}=\text{I} \right]$

or, $2\text{I}=\left( {{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\left( \text{sin}z-\text{cos}z \right) \right.$

$\therefore I=\frac{ez}{2}\left( \text{sin}z-\text{cos}z \right)+c$

$=\frac{x}{2}\left[ \text{sin}\left( \text{log}x \right)-\text{cos}\left( \text{log}x \right) \right]+c$

$\left[ \therefore {{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}=\text{x} \right.$ and $\left. \text{logx}=\text{z} \right]$

examples.title: If $\int x \sin x d x=-x \cos x+A$, then $A=$
$\int x e^x d x$ is equal to
